responseSet.title: Out of the mainstream
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Out of the mainstream

Liv Darling

Liv Darling

London
UK

Liv Darling  

Rosemary Loshak, London and New York, Routledge, 2013, 205 pp, RRP £24.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-0-415-68270-1

This book makes a significant contribution to the endeavour to help children whose parents suffer from mental illness. The editor is a psychoanalytic psychotherapist and social worker who has been at the forefront of developing a pioneering service for these children and their parents. Chapters written by professionals working from different perspectives present a picture of the development of a community service called the Children and Adult Mental Health Project (CHAMP). The book depicts the struggle to knit together different agencies in the common task of focussing on the needs of children whose difficulties have historically been difficult to address. The tendency is always to focus on the individual patient rather than the patient and their context, and the child's needs are often eclipsed by the apparently more acute needs of the parent(s) with a mental illness. The struggle to work across organisational boundaries is usefully complemented by the inclusion in the book of different theoretical perspectives but at the heart of the book is psychoanalytic thinking, whether applied to organisational issues or to clinical situations.

The authors of different chapters repeatedly comment that there is a huge unmet need for services. Research is quoted showing that one-third of the children of mentally ill parents are unaffected, one-third develop some difficulties and the remaining third suffer from very significant difficulties. In the UK, two million children are living with a parent or parents with mental illness. In the area where the service described was set up, Tower Hamlets in East London, the community mental health teams were working with approximately 500 adults of whom one-third were parents. The book contains vivid descriptions of the need voiced by children to be taken account of by the services concerned with the mental health of their parents. In a chapter entitled ‘What children say’, children describe their experiences of parents with mental illnesses: ‘Sometimes I'm like worried to leave her alone … even like when I went to school I always used to think about her’.

An exploration of core psychiatry trainees experience of and thoughts
	 surrounding personal psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An exploration of core psychiatry trainees experience of and thoughts surrounding personal psychotherapy

Shivanthi Sathanandan and Darren Bull

Darren Bull

London
UK
Department of Psychiatry

London
UK

Darren Bull  

psychiatric trainees, personal psychotherapy, qualitative research, medical education

A web-based survey was sent out to the core trainees 1–3 (CT1–3) at the University College London Partners training scheme in North London to explore their thoughts on the role of personal psychotherapy in psychiatry training. Information was asked regarding demographics, personal psychotherapy experience, thoughts about starting psychotherapy and how this could be supported by the training scheme. One-third of the respondents had undertaken personal psychotherapy. Most of them felt there had been a personal and professional benefit from it. Of the remaining trainees, the majority would consider it. The majority of trainees felt that it should be incorporated into some form in the curriculum. Subsidized cost, availability during work hours, taster sessions and more information available were suggestions of how to make it accessible to trainees. There is value in training schemes and psychotherapists increasing awareness of the benefits of psychotherapy in psychiatry. This will not only have a positive benefit to the patients we treat but also maintain our own well-being and prevent the all too common state of ‘burnout’ in psychiatrists.

responseSet.title: Engaging with climate change: psychoanalytic and interdisciplinary perspectives
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Engaging with climate change: psychoanalytic and interdisciplinary perspectives

Helga Skogstad

Helga Skogstad

Surrey
UK

Helga Skogstad  

S. Weintrobe. The New Library of Psychoanalysis ‘Beyond the Couch Series’, Hove, Routledge, 2013, xxii+255 pp., £ 27.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-0-415-66762-3

This is a rich and fascinating book that makes for gripping reading. It brings together different disciplines and is therefore interesting for a wide range of readers. There are contributions from psychoanalysts and psychotherapists from different orientations and working with different patient groups, alongside contributions from ecologists and climate scientists. To these are added contributions from climate activists and professionals with experience in advertising campaigns.

The book clearly explains psychoanalytic concepts, so that a reader not accustomed to this way of thinking can gain an understanding. At the same time, there are many accounts from the consulting room and from literature, so that it is also delightful reading for those trained in the field. Similarly, the reader who is less accustomed with the psycho-social and political approach, or less knowledgeable about effects of climate change will also be engaged by the well-written contributions from those fields. It is a book that combines psychoanalytic and interdisciplinary perspectives, as promised in the title, and does so in an extremely rich way. As all chapters are discussed by one or two people, and sometimes followed by a response from the author, the reader is helped to follow the arguments. The chapters lead coherently from one to the next.

Yet, in an emotional sense, it is not easy reading. I experienced in myself just those feelings described in the book: the difficulty of facing the destruction that has been done, the guilt of not doing enough and the conflict of not wanting to give up a comfortable life style that is detrimental and causes suffering to human beings and species elsewhere in the world.

Every chapter in the book contributes valuably to the whole and leaves a distinct impression. It is not possible to do justice to the complexity of the arguments, for which close reading of the book is required, but some key points of each chapter warrant mention. Sally Weintrobe, in her Introduction, writes that ‘it is increasingly clear that understanding human responses to climate change is just as important – if not more important – than understanding climate change itself’ (p. 1). Throughout, the book explores why, despite increasing evidence of the detrimental effects of climate change, this is not acted on sufficiently or is even denied.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley  

Whilst there continues to be an imperative for psychoanalytic psychotherapy to demonstrate its efficacy and cost-effectiveness in order for it to survive as a treatment offered within the public sector, too much of a focus on objective measurement and outcome may fail to capture the more subjective, yet vital, processes that occur in the intimacy of the therapeutic encounter. The papers in this issue, in their exploration of the unconscious dynamics underpinning the analytic process, challenge the prevailing paradigm of positivism and causal determinism in which all observations are assumed to be capable of being objectively defined, validated and applied reliably. Grappling with complex theoretical concepts, the authors attempt to capture the elusive and subjective nature of a deeply personal human contact, and in doing so, expose the limitations of over-reliance on objective measurement of the therapeutic method.

Thus, Penelope Crick, in her eloquently argued paper ‘Thinking about judgement in psychoanalytic assessment and consultation’, explores the nature of clinical judgement and decision-making in the psychotherapeutic assessment of patients. She considers both the external and internal pressures that may push ‘judgement’, which remains a flexible and open process, into a more rigid and closed system of ‘judgementalism’, which constrains and interferes with a fair valuation of the patient's capacities and difficulties. Crick demonstrates how unconscious subjectivity forms an essential element of the practice of psychoanalytic assessment, and how awareness of subjective processes facilitates negotiation of the ‘emotional storm’ between the two participants in the initial therapeutic encounter so that clinical judgement, rather than judgementalism, prevails.

Noreen Giffney continues the theme of the therapist's receptivity to the patient's communications in her paper ‘Bion's discourses of desire’. Taking Bion's much quoted saying ‘without memory and desire’, Giffney's close reading and interpretation of Bion's writings on this subject explores what it means exactly for the therapist to be without desire, how this comes about and the consequences of such a stance. Exploration of these questions is important as Bion's dictum risks becoming applied in a literal and formulaic fashion without being fully understood. Giffney quotes David Bell in his warning that ‘[Bion's] advice that analysts should eschew memory and desire, a poetic expression of Freud's “evenly-suspended attention”, is easily transformed into a near-oracular statement to be worshipped in a cult-like manner’ (Bell, 2011, p. 99). responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley  

The advent of 2013 may appear to bring little relief from the continuing economic constraints, reorganisation of mental health services and uncertainty regarding the future of psychoanalytic psychotherapy within the public sector. In the UK, the commissioning of all psychological therapies is being re-thought and the phrases ‘evidence based practice’, ‘treatment efficacy’ and ‘patient outcomes’ are foremost in the vocabulary of commissioners. It is therefore encouraging that psychoanalytically informed work, as described in the four main papers in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, continues to be delivered and developed despite, or perhaps in some cases, because of, these current challenges. Although describing work with different patient populations in different settings and indeed countries (a General Practitioner practice, an in-patient eating disorder unit in the UK, a subsidised psychotherapy clinic for young people in Sweden and a psychoanalytic practice treating English-speaking émigrés in Germany), all of the articles may be read and appreciated with this uneasy backdrop of environmental change in mind.

A common thread running through these papers, and a concept that is so fundamental to our work but is at risk of being lost in the current climate, is that of attachment. Inspired by the work of Balint, Smith in his paper describes the fragmentation caused by recent changes in the delivery of counselling and psychological therapies within primary care and makes a compelling argument for the continued centrality of the counsellor's role as an integral part of individual GP practices in providing effective short-term therapeutic work from a psychodynamic perspective. Fostering the patient's trust and developing therapeutic alliances between not only patient and therapist but also at a more systemic level in the multiple relationships between patient, counsellor and the GP practice as a whole allow the patient to experience the GP practice as a secure base in which therapeutic regression emerging into psychic growth can safely occur. This may remind us (and our commissioners!) that one of the most consistent findings in psychotherapy outcome research studies is the correlation between the strength of the therapeutic alliance and positive therapeutic outcome. This finding is replicated in the qualitative study described by Palmstierna and Werbart in this issue, which explores patients' views and experiences of what leads to therapeutic process and outcome in longer term psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The therapeutic relationship and feeling safe due to the continuity and therapeutic frame provided by the therapist were the most commonly cited factors that patients found helpful, leading the authors to conclude that a growth-promoting and secure relationship forms the basis to all therapeutic work. responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Heather Wood and Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley  

The Internet has been widely accessible to the public for nearly two decades. However, until now the psychoanalytic community has been almost silent about its use, with Gabbard's (2001) early paper a notable exception. Within the psychoanalytic world, the maintenance of a secure frame for the psychotherapeutic encounter has been seen as an absolutely essential cornerstone of practice, allowing transference and countertransference processes to develop and be examined. This may have led those experimenting with the frame by offering therapy or supervision by telephone or voice over Internet protocol to be discreet about their practice, for fear of being seen to flout a fundamental principle of psychoanalysis, and, so, to risk being accused of not practising analytically.

This year has seen the publication of two edited collections of papers on the subject of the Internet and its place in psychoanalytic practice, Scharff's (2013) Psychoanalysis Online, and Lemma and Capparotta's (2014) Psychoanalysis in the Technoculture Era. These very interesting collections, both reviewed in this Special Issue, bring the subject out of the consulting room and into the area of open debate. Scharff reports a recent study by the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA, 2011) which found that 28% of respondents reported using the phone and 9% using Skype™ for psychotherapy, and 9% using the phone and 4% using Skype™ for psychoanalysis. Online supervision, online analysis or ‘teletherapy’ by phone may still be practised by a minority of therapists, but they are part of the repertoire of available treatments and current practice. There are issues to consider, for our professional community as a whole and for each individual practitioner, about whether and how to participate in this development.

Scharff's collection, and many of the papers in Lemma and Caparrotta, relates to the provision of private psychoanalytic therapy, supervision and training. Scharff's collection also includes chapters by psychotherapists who themselves have experienced therapy using the telephone or Internet, for example, the chapter by Anderson (2013) reflecting on his experience of a four times weekly training analysis by telephone. Both collections of papers include many illustrations and examples of the constructive use of telephone and Internet contact between patient and analyst or therapist, and illustrations of lively transference and countertransference processes in operation. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Successful psychotherapies with young adults: an explorative study of the participants' view

Vendela Palmstierna and Andrzej Werbart

Andrzej Werbart

PO Box 179 14
SE-118 95
Stockholm
Sweden

SE-106 91
Stockholm
Sweden
Department of Psychology

BUF
SE-141 85
Huddinge
Sweden

Andrzej Werbart  

successful cases, process and outcome, young adults, psychoanalytic psychotherapy

In order to understand mechanisms of change, patients' experiences are an invaluable source of information. Psychotherapy with young adults confronts therapists with specific challenges. This qualitative study explores experiences of therapeutic process and outcome in 11 cases of psychoanalytic psychotherapy that fulfilled the rigorous quantitative criteria for long-term therapeutic success. Interviews at termination and at a 1.5-year follow-up were analysed applying basic grounded theory methodology and the software ATLAS.ti. At the core of the tentative conceptual model is the experience of a growth-promoting and secure therapeutic relationship. Within the therapeutic frames, the patients and the therapists could overcome obstacles to their collaboration. The patients obtained support in close relationships and continued to apply therapeutic experiences after termination. The therapists experienced the therapeutic work in a strikingly similar way, worked actively towards joint goals, explored what was painful and actively promoted the use of new skills after termination. Positive factors in therapy and in life reinforced each other in a positive feedback loop. The patients' and their therapists' view of successful change processes confirms the centrality of the therapeutic relationship as a secure base for exploration and change.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The practice of individual psychodynamic psychotherapy with people who have intellectual disabilities

Tom Jackson and Nigel Beail

Nigel Beail

Barnsley
UK

Sheffield
UK
Clinical Psychology Unit

Nigel Beail  

psychodynamic psychotherapy, intellectual disability, psychoanalysis, formulation, psychotherapy practice, psychotherapy

Problem: Individual psychodynamic psychotherapy has become more widely available to people who have intellectual disabilities in recent years and a literature on its use has developed. However, there does not appear to be a clear exposition of the process and practice of this treatment approach when used with this population. Method: Case studies and technical papers relating to the process and practice of individual psychodynamic psychotherapy with people with intellectual disabilities published between 1981 and 2010 were examined to ascertain how the model was being used. Practice was delineated into the nature of the therapeutic frame and the action of therapists within sessions. Outcome: The nature of the therapeutic frame was discussed with reference to the available literature, noting the need for flexibility around settings and session duration whilst maintaining the consistency of the frame required, ensuring provision of a secure base. The action of therapists was considered in three stages: information gathering, formulation/ recontextualisation and communication of interpretations. Discussion: Common practice was identified whilst acknowledging the paucity of descriptive practice in the literature, particularly with regard to the formulation of material into interpretations. Areas for development are identified to develop a literature to assist with developing practice and training.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative readings: essays on seminal analytic works

Carlos Fishman

Carlos Fishman

London
UK

Carlos Fishman  

Thomas H. Ogden. The New Library of Psychoanalysis series, London, Routledge, 2012, 261 pp., £26.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-0-415-69833-7

Ogden, the well-known, original and prolific American psychoanalyst who has been heavily influenced by object relations theory, offers us an interesting experience in this, his latest book. He has selected a number of important papers and has undertaken the task of reading them and annotating them with his understanding, his thinking and his comments, adding at times clinical material from his own work that leads us through the journey of his own reading of these papers. We all know that when we read a paper it stimulates our own thoughts. Ogden invites us to engage with the thoughts which he has in response to these classic papers and in that way invites us to look at any given essay anew, while also having a window onto the mind of a very creative psychoanalytic thinker. He pays particular attention to language, particularly the phrasing and structure of certain sentences, and puts them ‘under the microscope’, exploring how this or that way of saying something gives a certain connotation to the idea, and the thoughts that it conjures up in him. He also defines and expands on the concept of ‘analytic style’, a most interesting concept that refers to each particular analyst's way of practicing analysis.

He starts with a very scholarly and careful annotation of Freud's ‘Mourning and melancholia’ offering an original perspective on one of Freud's central ideas in this paper: the way in which ‘the shadow of the object’ affects the ego. His notes on the central influence of this paper on Freud's theory of narcissism are very stimulating and invite serious thought: he proposes that the ‘lost object’ for the melancholic is, in effect, ‘abandoned’, rather than as might be assumed, ‘abandoning’. This very interesting way of seeing it, I think, adds a particular dimension to Freud's notion of the ambivalent relationship that disturbs normal mourning and develops into melancholia.

Initiating psychoanalysis

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

London

Jessica Yakeley  

Bernard Reith, Sven Lagerlöf, Penelope Crick, Mette Møller and Elisabeth Skale. The New Library of Psychoanalysis Teaching Series, Series Editor: Dana Birksted-Breen, London, Routledge, 359 pp., ISBN 978-0-415-55498-5

We all have personal stories to tell about our own introduction to the world of psychoanalysis, and although this book is written ostensibly for clinicians immersed in psychoanalytic work with their patients, it inevitably resonates with ourselves as patients, specifically evoking our own memories of our first meaningful psychoanalytic experiences that have necessarily remained with us to form the foundations upon which all of our subsequent work has been built. This book is about psychoanalytic beginnings, the initiation of the psychoanalytic process, a critical phase which offers the opportunity not just for the first psychoanalytic encounter for a prospective patient but for a unique meeting of two people – patient and analyst – to create something that has not existed before. In parallel fashion, the book is a collection of mostly previously published papers on the subject, but by bringing them together and drawing out both complementary and conflicting themes, it creates something new and worthy of our attention.

The book evolved out of the work of a working party of the European Psychoanalytic Federation that was investigating the theoretical, clinical and technical issues involved in the initiation of psychoanalytic treatment, in particular the dynamics of first interviews. Their aim was to represent a wide scope of linguistic and psychoanalytic cultures, as well as a range of settings in which psychoanalytic treatment is practiced. The resulting volume spans both geographical and temporal dimensions in being a collection of papers from authors working in different countries and at different stages on the history of psychoanalysis, starting with Freud's first encounter with his patient ‘Katharina’ on a mountain top in the Alps in the early 1890s. The book's structure respects these boundaries of location and history but also brings together common themes within its respective subsections such as historical overview, consultation and referral, and the analyst's experiences and difficulties. Each section has a foreword by one of the editors (each representing a different European country themselves), and each paper is preceded by an invited commentary about its author who also situates it within its historical and cultural context. This helps the reader in deciphering some of the less accessible chapters which focus on metapsychology rather than providing examples of direct clinical experience as do some of the more readable chapters. responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley and Laura Allison

Laura Allison

Laura Allison  

As practitioners of psychoanalytic psychotherapy the bread and butter of our quotidian work involves thinking, reflecting, feeling and focusing on our inner worlds and those of our patients, and we prioritise reflective forums in which we may continue this thinking about our patients with our colleagues. Yet for some individuals the very act of thinking is threatening as it risks putting the person in contact with fantasies, memories and affective states that may destabilize their psychic, and sometimes even physical, equilibrium. All of the papers in this issue are linked in their focus on applying psychoanalytic ideas to conditions - learning disabilities, psychosomatic illnesses, addictions and obsessive compulsive disorder - that many have considered resistant to psychoanalytic intervention. These resistances may take different forms, but at their roots are fundamental deficits and defences against thinking. Such resistances, however, may not only lie with the patients but may also be located in the therapists and clinicians in their reluctance to treat these patients who are difficult to engage, act out their impulses in risky ways, or express their distress in bodily symptoms and concerns; patients who seem unable to hold on to and reflect on their inner lives, and who engender complex countertransference reactions which may become manifest in beliefs that such patients are not amenable to a psychoanalytic approach at all. This therapeutic pessimism may be strengthened by the prevailing biological and cognitive paradigms that consider such conditions are best managed by medication or by solely focusing on the removal of symptoms and behaviours, rather than a more exploratory psychodynamic intervention.

It is therefore heartening that the papers in this issue collectively put forward convincing evidence that psychoanalytic thinking may offer a valuable contribution to conceptualizing and treating, albeit sometimes with a modified approach, patients with conditions and disorders that traditionally might only be referred for psychoanalytic or psychodynamic psychotherapy as a last resort, if at all. Thus, in the first paper, Tom Jackson and Nigel Beail investigate the practice of individual psychodynamic psychotherapy with people who have intellectual disabilities, a population that many people assert do not benefit from a traditional model of psychotherapy. Nevertheless, there exists a notable literature concerning psychodynamic psychotherapy with people with intellectual disabilities, which the authors review and from which they distil the nature and process of a psychodynamic approach for this population and identify areas for further development. Continued curiosity and innovation are vital to sustain public sector psychotherapy services through this challenging time, and this paper is an example of how one can re-evaluate current thinking, and find new avenues for exploration.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis online: mental health, teletherapy and training

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley   Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

The psychoanalytic world has been slow to openly engage with the possibilities afforded by new technologies of communication. Although use of the telephone in psychoanalysis (‘teleanalysis’) first appeared in the psychoanalytic literature in the 1950s, subsequent publications on the subject were scarce until the turn of the last century. This literary silence seems unlikely to reflect the actual prevalence of teleanalysis in psychoanalytic work, but is more likely the result of therapists' anxieties of being judged or criticised by colleagues for deviating beyond accepted clinical theory and practice.

It is, therefore, heartening to witness this year the publication of not one, but two books addressing the interface between psychoanalytic therapy and the telephone and Internet. Scharff's (2013) Psychoanalysis Online: Mental Health, Teletherapy and Training, the subject of this review, and Psychoanalysis in the Technoculture Era, by Lemma and Caparrotta (2014; also reviewed in this issue), both explore the questions and controversies raised by the use of technology in the consulting room. It seems improbable that the simultaneous publication of these two volumes is coincidental, but is more likely that it is due to a growing wave of psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic psychotherapists interested in the influence of social media and cyberspace on society, and open to adaptation of their theory and technique to incorporate these communication technologies in the best interests of patients.

Psychoanalysis Online, edited by Jill Savege Scharff, a psychoanalyst and psychotherapist based in Maryland, USA, is a collection of papers that emerged from an international workgroup of colleagues from the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA) and the International Institute for Psychoanalytic Training (IIPT) studying the practice of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy conducted on the telephone and over the Internet. The chapters are written by psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic therapists mostly working in the USA, but also in other countries across the world including Kazakhstan, Germany, Argentina, Panama and Canada. Several chapters are by qualified therapists who retrospectively describe their experiences of teleanalysis as candidates in training, adding an authentic tone of first-hand experiences of the advantages and limitations of this mode of therapy. [[RunningHead]]

Challenges in treating patients with psychosomatic disorders: some patterns of resistance

Jaime Yasky , Robert King and Tom O'Brien

Tom O'Brien

Brisbane
Australia

Brisbane
Australia

Tom O'Brien  

psychosomatic disorders, resistance, defences, narcissistic transference, counter-transference resistance

The treatment of people suffering from psychosomatic disorders poses important clinical challenges to psychoanalytic practice, including early abandonment, difficulties in engagement and superficial involvement in the treatment. The lack of systematic research on the subject suggests that a better understanding of the resistances and dynamics involved in such episodes is needed. Using supervision group to study treatments and inspired by action-research paradigm, this paper presents the results of an 18-month mixed-method qualitative clinical research on treating 22 cases. Thematic analysis revealed a frequent pattern of resistance to experience emotions that presented in two states: failure of repression (thin-skin) and massive repression (thick-skin). Narcissistic dynamics in the therapeutic relationship taking the form of ‘resistance to transference’ and a tendency to action were evident in both presentations. Symmetric and complementary counter-transference reactions, which compromised treatment, were also identified. Some technical implications are highlighted, such as the need to focus in the recovery of the patient's actual emotional experience and to understand such experience in the context of the ‘here and now’ of the therapeutic encounter. These findings suggest the need for a flexible approach to therapeutic work that moves between an ‘intrapsychic’ and a ‘relational’ focus.

Nurturing natures. Attachment and children's emotional, sociocultural and brain development

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

Devon
UK

Jeremy Holmes  

Graham Music, Psychology Press, 2011, 311 pp. (paperback)

Music to our ears

Hold onto your seats for a rave review.

One of the countless gems in this goldmine of a book (slightly to mix a metaphor) shows how negative and positive appraisals operate as separate bio-behavioural systems. It is not that happy people do not suffer, just that their positive experiences outnumber and outweigh the negative in the sense of feeling stronger and more salient. In addition, our sensitivity to the latter is far greater than to the positive. In his amazing range of reference, Music unearths' studies showing that human taste buds can detect one part in 2 m of bitterness, but only 1:200 sweetness. It is better to be bitterly alive than cheerfully dead, but – this is the virtue of the gift of secure attachment – better still to be happily alive. As an appraiser – or perhaps an app-praise-er – this book succeeds magnificently, both ways. Not only is it enjoyable, uplifting and hugely informative, but also – within its chosen terms of reference, faultless – making life hard for an habitually nit-picking reviewer!

Music's project is to survey the current state of knowledge – theoretical and experimental – in child development from conception through to adolescence. If it is not too clunky a comparison, a good book – and this is a very good book – is like a sturdy tree: firm structure of trunk and branches apposite to their ecological niche/genre; graceful, illuminating leaves; a floral aroma of inspirational overtones; fruit-bearing for the next generation. Music's book has just such qualities, with five sections, each offering a clear structure: early days, mainly about mother–infant interaction; developmental theories, biological, attachment based and sociological; themes, in which he surveys a number of key topics such as play, language and gender; non-maternal care and the role of ‘alloparents’, fathers, siblings, foster parents and finally psychopathology, how and why things go wrong, and what can be done about it. At each stage and sub-section, he reviews the state-of-the-art knowledge and theories, deftly summarising findings and weaving them into a comprehensible story without the tendentiousness (cherry-picking facts that fit particular prejudices) that bedevils our field.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in the technoculture era

Brian Rock

Brian Rock

Brian Rock   Email: brock@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Psychoanalysis in the Technoculture Era (edited by Alessandra Lemma and Luigi Caparrotta) will no doubt become an influential text for theorists and practitioners alike who are interested in, concerned by, and engaged with the intersection of psychoanalysis and the significant changes taking place in the development and use of technology in our everyday lives. These changes are not only profoundly influencing how we communicate and relate, but also significantly shaping the very nature of communication and relationships. At the beginning of his foreword, Peter Fonagy questions whether ‘the technological revolution of information technology [has] brought about an irreversible shift in our psychological world’ (p. xv). He goes on to trace the epistemological shifts in the instinctual models of psychoanalysis from those originating with ‘sex and aggression’, to attachment theory and now to the instinct related to the ‘transfer of knowledge’ (p. xviii). And, he appears to answer his original question, by saying that it is this last instinct that is ‘altered in its essence, not just in its expression or its manifestations’ (p. xviii).

Lemma and Caparrotta respond by bringing together a collection of writers, each with their own voice and perspective, yet lightly linked together. The resulting offerings in this slim volume are thought-provoking and emotionally engaging, which made it both a pleasure and a challenge to read. Not at first, but as I read more of the chapters, I found myself involved in a conversation with the authors and myself. What did I really think about the issues under discussion? What had I not considered? What did I need to know? I particularly appreciated the fact that a number of writers expressed ideas that were in contradiction, which added to the richness and internal conversation, and showed the freedom given by the editors to write about what they were truly interested in and engaged by. This has made for an engaging book.

Their introduction was comprehensive and catered for different audiences from the less technologically proficient reader to those that would be more au fait with the subject matter, throughout ensuring that the psychoanalytic relevance was explored with a balanced tone. Lemma and Caparrotta neither adopted a stance of caution and condemnation nor were too seduced by the possibilities afforded by technological advance. They helpfully distinguished between the ‘tools’ and ‘the uses’ these tools are put to. In so doing, they balanced potential development and opportunities arising from technological advances with the possible onset of difficulties leading to pathology. With respect to any technical 344

responseSet.title: Thoughts without a thinker, mimetic fusing and the anti-container considered as primitive defensive mechanisms in the addictions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts without a thinker, mimetic fusing and the anti-container considered as primitive defensive mechanisms in the addictions

Alistair Sweet

Alistair Sweet

Belfast

Alistair Sweet  

containment, mimetic, fusing, projective, identification, objects

This paper explores certain primitive defensive mechanisms found with addicted patients and introduces one of these, an intra-psychic mechanism, termed mimetic fusing. The descriptive concept of mimetic fusing is introduced in order to illuminate the processes by which a primitive self appears to repeat attempts to assimilate and detoxify introjections that are felt to be threatening and that generate intense anxiety. It is suggested that in the absence of an adequate container (Bion, 1962. A theory of thinking. International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, 43(4-5)), mimetic fusing may develop as a defensive mechanism to assimilate aggressively imbued introjections that threaten to overwhelm a fragile self-structure. The author suggests that mimetic fusing may represent a desperate defensive strategy that ultimately leads to the increased use of other basic defences such as splitting, the anti-container, anti-time and projective identification. Case material, taken from work with an addicted patient in longer term weekly psychotherapy, is included to illustrate the theoretical points under discussion.

responseSet.title: The perception of digital objects and their impact on development
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The perception of digital objects and their impact on development

Richard Graham

Richard Graham

Richard Graham   *Email: rgraham@tavi-port.nhs.uk
digital, Internet, mobile, media, suicide

Clinical work with adolescents and young people over the last decade has indicated that an intense relationship to digital objects was a major aspect of their mental lives. Digital objects have become embedded, inextricably, within our psychology, emphasising the earliest drives and phantasies at some moments, yet giving rise to less familiar phenomena at other times. The aim of this paper is to consider how the rise in digital media and social networking is impacting upon the lives of young people and influencing the course of their development, with particular reference to the impact of visual media within social networks and how this has a distinct influence on mental functioning. In attempting to describe these processes, it is hoped that we can become more able to accompany younger or older people in their exploration of the digital world, which will be as important to them in their future adult lives as their mastery of the physical, external world was in early childhood. The need is pressing, since access to the digital world has significantly increased through the use of mobile, connected devices, and the further evolution of new technologies, such as ‘the internet of everything’ and ‘wearable tech’, are almost upon us.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A base for a new beginning: benign regression in a GP practice

Jonathan Smith

Jonathan Smith

30 Russell Square
London
WC1B 5DT
UK
Department of Psychosocial Studies

Jonathan Smith  

secure base, benign and malignant regression, new beginning, milieu therapy, independent discoveries

The author describes the function of the General Practitioner (GP) practice in providing a secure base which can promote and sustain the development of a working alliance in the therapeutic work of counsellors practicing in the setting. The contribution of the counsellor in facilitating the development of a secure base through the relationships and reflective spaces that are established with GPs and other health care professionals is highlighted. Therapeutic techniques deriving from the work of Michael Balint, which foster an essential equality in the therapeutic relationship and value the patient's independent discoveries are outlined, and a case example illustrates how an analytic attitude based on these principles can on occasions result in a benign regression that results in a New Beginning for the patient. The GP setting which provides a secure base and an opportunity for milieu therapy is shown to have made a significant contribution to the therapeutic process.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and ecology at the edge of chaos: complexity theory, Deleuze/Guattari and psychoanalysis for a climate in crisis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and ecology at the edge of chaos: complexity theory, Deleuze/Guattari and psychoanalysis for a climate in crisis

Robert Tollemache

Robert Tollemache

London
UK

Robert Tollemache  

Joseph Dodds, Routledge, 2011, 238 pp., £80 (hardback), £19.99 (paperback). ISBN 978-0-415-66611-4

What does the environment have to do with psychoanalysis?

In 1972, Harold Searles challenged the psychoanalytic world when he suggested that the lack of environmental thinking within psychoanalysis might be caused by destructive unconscious factors. Since his call for a psychoanalytic environmental debate, until Dodds's response, the psychoanalytic movement has remained largely silent.

Martin Jordan describes Joseph Dodds in the book's preface as a ‘nomadic scholar’; he is training as a psychoanalyst with the Czech Psychoanalytical Society, and lectures at the University of New York in Prague. His book springs from an MPhil thesis. In this review, I will describe how Dodds understands the relationship between psychoanalysis and climate change and how he sees complexity theory and self-organisation as adding to psychoanalytic understanding.

Dodds maintains that ‘the new nomadic sciences of complexity combined with the philosophy of Deleuze and Guattari help to provide a framework…. for thinking our way out of extinction’ (p. 183). He believes that the crisis of potential future climate catastrophe calls for the introduction of non-linear, ecological thinking to psychoanalysis; the environment, he argues, is present but silent in psychoanalysis. Balint describes ‘a harmonious interpenetrating mix-up’ (Balint, 1968, p. 66), where the object is taken for granted, as a fish swims in the sea, just as we assume the ongoing supply of air. Psychoanalysis, lacking a conceptual framework for thinking about the medium in which we live, has disregarded environmental issues.

Dodds suggests ways of understanding psychoanalytic ideas and their relationship with nature and the environment. Freud, he proposes, saw civilisation as opposed to nature; Deleuze and Guattari see mind, society and nature as a continuum, separated only for clarity and convenience; Dodds shows us the interconnectedness between the ecology of mind, the ecology of society and the ecology of nature; each, worlds of infinite complexity, and locates psychoanalysis as part of a mind–society–nature continuum. This interconnectedness with nature contrasts with our psychoanalytic practice, where we try to exclude the outside world and to provide a secure frame through dependability, calm, confidentiality and continuity within the consulting room. In the changing world, we provide the illusion of stability.

[[RunningHead]]

Mirror mirror on the ward: who is the unfairest of them all? Reflections on reflective practice groups in acute psychiatric settings

James Johnston and Graham Paley

Graham Paley

Southfield House, 40 Clarendon Road
Leeds
LS2 9PJ
UK

Graham Paley  

reflective practice groups, mental health, psychotherapy, in-patients, management, the do do bird

Consultant psychiatrists in medical psychotherapy, adult psychotherapists, child and adolescent psychotherapists and clinical psychologists increasingly complement their direct therapeutic activity with applications of their psychotherapeutic thinking in acute mental health work through facilitating reflective practice groups for staff working in mental health teams. The authors offer their reflections on facilitating National Health Service reflective practice groups using the metaphor of a mirrored dialogue between patient and professional, and professional and institution as a basis for informing the development of reflective practice for colleagues. Their reflections are based on working on three acute in-patient wards, in a crisis resolution team and in community mental health teams. They describe the practicalities of setting up and facilitating reflective practice groups, and offer insights into some of the issues that arise in reflective practice groups. They conclude that these groups are mutually beneficial in forging links between psychotherapy professionals and professionals working in other disciplines and areas of mental health. Facilitating these groups often requires a challenging adaptation of technique, which will not suit all psychotherapists, as well as a wider understanding of organisational dynamics and the interplay between clinicians and management.

Thinking about judgement in psychoanalytic assessment and consultation

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick

Byron House, Shirland Road
London
W9 2EQ
UK

Penelope Crick  

subjective judgement, judgementalism, clinical judgement, psychoanalytic consultation and assessment, doubt, negative capability

The role and nature of clinical judgement in psychoanalytic consultation are discussed and illustrated with clinical material from psychoanalytic consultations. The considerable and inevitable pressures that act on ‘judgement’ and ‘judgementalism’ are considered. A model for psychoanalytic consultation that takes these pressures into account is described, showing how the subjectivity of clinical judgement is an essential element in understanding and decision-making. Internal as well as external pressures on the formation and proper valuation of psychoanalytic clinical judgement are noted.

responseSet.title: Internet pornography and paedophilia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internet pornography and paedophilia

Heather Wood

Heather Wood

Portman Clinic

Heather Wood   *Email: hwood@tavi-port.nhs.uk
perversion, Internet sex, adult sexual adaptation, child pornography

Various indices of use of Internet pornography have drawn attention to the extent of adult sexual interest in children; clinical experience and now research evidence are accumulating to suggest that the Internet is not simply drawing attention to those with existing paedophilic interests, but is contributing to the crystallisation of those interests in people with no explicit prior sexual interest in children. Drawing on clinical experience in a specialist NHS outpatient psychotherapy service, the author argues that a dichotomous notion of paedophilia is no longer tenable, and any model of paedophilia needs to take account of eruptions of a sexual interest in children in those who appear not to be consistently paedophilic and who have had adult-to-adult sexual relationships. This phenomenon is analysed in two ways: first, by considering how it is that the adult sexual adaptation might ‘unravel’ under the influence of Internet sex to reveal underlying paedophilic currents and second, by revisiting general and psychoanalytic theories of paedophilia and by considering how predisposing factors to paedophilia might be amplified through access to Internet pornography.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two types of psychic encapsulation in anorexia

Yael Kadish

Yael Kadish

South Africa
Department of Psychology

Yael Kadish  

anorexia, autistoid, encapsulation, pathological organisation, psychotherapy, psychic retreat

This paper suggests that it is theoretically necessary and clinically useful to make a distinction between two types of psychic encapsulation within the broader literature. The proposed distinction, as it relates to these psychic structural manifestations, is illustrated here as applied to anorexia. The author suggests that psychic encapsulation is commonly encountered in work with anorexics, and that each type seems to imply a somewhat different therapeutic course. The distinction is made between anorexic patients who appear to display evidence of autistic/autistoid encapsulation as opposed to those who seem to manifest non-autistoid/later traumatic encapsulation – termed secondary adjunctive encapsulation in this paper. Defensive encapsulations are associated with pathological organisations of the personality – both within and beyond these structures, they exert an organising power over central mental processes. Psychic encapsulation and pathological organisations are defensive structural developments – the result of psychic trauma. Clinical material from three cases is presented to illustrate the arguments.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Desire in psychoanalytic psychotherapy: the writings of W.R. Bion

Noreen Giffney

Noreen Giffney

Dublin
Ireland

Noreen Giffney  

Bion, desire, discourse, caesura, clinical technique

This paper considers the place of desire in psychoanalytic psychotherapy, by undertaking a close reading of some of Wilfred Bion's writings on the subject. Focusing on his dictum that therapists should enter their consulting rooms without desire, this paper provides an overview of why Bion believes that the therapist's foregoing of desire is conducive to psychoanalytic work. This paper outlines his preference for primary ignorance, negative capability and acts of faith to facilitate the therapist experiencing what is going on in the here and now as fully as possible. This paper argues that Bion's concept of the caesura is useful for thinking about how the transition from being with desire to being without desire might take place for the therapist.

Innovations in simulated and technology-aided learning for core psychiatric trainees in psychotherapy

Salvatore Mura , Hayley Ponsford , Jale Punter , Aman Sardana , Sheetal Sirohi and Jenissa Tanna

Jenissa Tanna

Walthamstow
London
UK
Psychotherapy Department

London
UK

Epsom

Jenissa Tanna  

training, assessment of psychotherapy, SteLI

A training need to help core trainees prepare for the psychotherapy long case was identified. We prepared and showed a training DVD to all core psychiatric trainees at South West London and St Georges Mental Health NHS trust. A questionnaire was designed to receive feedback from trainees. A total of 26 trainees participated. All trainees found the DVD informative. Most trainees found the DVD helpful in reducing their anxieties prior to taking on a long case. All trainees found the DVD accurately reflected their experience of psychotherapy and supervision. Using simulated and technology-aided learning material is a useful and economical way of introducing psychotherapy skills to core psychiatry trainees before the clinical supervised experience of a long case.

Earl Hopper and Haim Weinberg, , London, New International Library of Group Analysis, Karnac Publishing, 2011, 344 pp. (paperback and eBook)

In the foreword to the book, Hopper reminds us that the main differentiator between group analysis and psychoanalysis is that psychoanalysis concerns the mind of the individual, whilst group analysis is concerned with the individual in relation to his social context. As such, group analytic work involves consideration of aspects of our socialized life in the form of class, language, race, ethnicity, culture, social systems and organization processes. Although the social unconscious has been a concept originally coined by Hopper and subsequently referred to within group analysis, here Hopper and Weinberg revisit the phenomenon in a series of papers written by a number of group therapists from around the world, most of whom have trainings that include group analysis.

The papers aim to provide a theoretical exploration of the social unconscious from beyond that which was originally provided by Hopper. The editors invited a number of different authors to provide chapters, and 3 years later the works have been organized in three volumes of which this is Volume I, ‘Mainly Theory’. Volume II is ‘Mainly Matrices’ and Volume III is ‘Mainly Clinical Work’.

Volume I, ‘Mainly Theory’, consists of six parts, each relating to a particular aspect of the social unconscious. Following the introduction, Part I, ‘The Origins of the Concept of the Social Unconscious’, provides an outline of early writings in group psychotherapy by its forerunners and founders and how an understanding of a social unconscious evolved.

Part II is rather interestingly titled ‘The Organismic and Neurobiological Perspective’. The inclusion of a section relating the psychological theory to neurobiology was in my opinion a great asset to this book, even if this, as with the other sections, was brief and only consisting of two chapters. Part III is ‘The Relational and Interpersonal Perspective’. Here the chapters describe the social unconscious in relation to inter-relationalality and intersubjectivity. Part IV, ‘The Mind of the Social System’, deals with political and social conflicts in international settings over two papers. As the heading of Part V suggests, ‘The Matrix of the Social System’, it deals with the core concept of group analysis, the matrix. Foulkes, as the godfather of group analysis, appeared to have difficulty describing the matrix succinctly and effectively in his own written work. Dual training in medical psychotherapy and psychiatry: advanced trainee perspectives

Vikram Luthra , Anuradha Menon , Shibani Mundra , Shari Mysorekar , Aleksandar Pavlovic and Phil Osborne

Phil Osborne

Southfield House, 40 Clarendon Road
Leeds

Phil Osborne  

medical psychotherapy, psychiatry, forensic, therapy

The medical psychotherapy integrated dual training programme in Yorkshire started in 2006. Over five years, the trainees achieve competencies simultaneously in medical psychotherapy and either in general adult or forensic psychiatry. The programme is structured in a way that trainees move between the two subspecialties within the working week, for the duration of the training. There are currently six advanced dual trainees (two of whom are less than full time). To give the reader a glimpse of this training, we have presented our individual accounts from six different vantage points. Our descriptions hopefully convey that supervision and personal therapy help hold this training together. We feel that an overall advantage of this integrated training is that we have an up-to-date knowledge and experience of both specialities along with a longer period of development within personal therapy. Whichever path we pursue, either as a psychiatrist or medical psychotherapist, we will offer additional qualities to the post. In the ever-changing National Health Service (NHS) of today, we understand how vital this will be to remain credible as consultants. For those of us who wish to further a career in psychoanalysis, an external training may be a viable option for the future.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group analytic therapy for compulsive users of internet pornography

John Woods

John Woods

John Woods   *Corresponding author. Email: JWoods@tavi-port.nhs.uk
group psychotherapy, internet pornography, sex addiction, perversion, psychodynamic model

Increasing numbers of patients presenting problems with internet pornography have led to changes in the culture and practice of group analytic therapy at the Portman Clinic. The addictive element of this new form of artificial, sexual stimulation has to be recognised as part of the harm it produces. Group members spontaneously develop some of the principles and practice of established methods of addiction treatment, especially the 12-step programme, by helping each other manage their compulsion. This self-help function of the group coexists alongside the psychodynamic perspective, in which the meaning of harmful sexual behaviour is explored and understood. Certain individuals are more vulnerable to excessive use of pornography because of early developmental problems and current difficulties in their sexual relationships, resulting from internal conflicts about their sexuality. The group provides intimacy without sexualisation, primarily through a sibling transference. The therapist embodies an ability to think about the complexity of the sexual abuse that the pornography portrays. He or she works towards a truer perception of reality for group members, in which the false solutions of compulsive sexual behaviour are seen to be what they are: impediments to healthy development.

Richard Agass

Heights Lane
Bradford

Richard Agass  

melancholic, antilibidinal, separation, self-attack, narcissism, sadomasochism

This paper reflects on the clinical phenomena of mental and physical self-attack as encountered in everyday psychotherapeutic practice in the NHS. The author considers two distinct but closely related internal dynamics which he terms ‘melancholic’ and ‘antilibidinal’. In both, there is a sadomasochistic structure which serves a number of defensive purposes for the individual. The origins and functioning of these structures are explored in the clinical material. These defensive systems are often perversely rewarding for the patient and highly resistant to change. The author discusses some of the major obstacles to working therapeutically in this area, and emphasizes the role of the transference and countertransference in helping the therapist to understand who is doing what to whom within the therapeutic relationship and within the patient. Although the paper deals with theoretical issues the emphasis throughout is on clinical understanding and effectiveness within a (mainly) once-weekly analytic setting.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When migration is used as a defence against painful realities: Some experiences of working with English-speaking patients in Germany

Kristin White

Kristin White

Senegalstr. 10
Berlin
13351
Germany

Kristin White  

migration, insecure attachment, psychic retreats, depressive position, Money-Kyrle's ‘facts of life’

In this paper, I discuss three small observations that I have made in the course of my years of working with English-speaking patients in Berlin. First, I will show how the experience of moving from one country to another tends to increase the likelihood of the use of primitive defence mechanisms as a protection against the difficulties of everyday life and in relationships in the new country. Second, I suggest that moving around the world has become a significant component of modern living in a globalised world. The danger of this social development is an increase in the use of splitting and in the use of ‘psychic retreats’ [Steiner, J. (1993). Psychic retreats. London: Routledge] in an attempt to uphold an idealised inner and outer world without pain or conflict. Third, I would like to underline the importance in psychoanalysis of helping the patient to mourn the losses he has experienced (the loss of his home country but also inner losses), and accepting the ‘facts of life’ [Money-Kyrle, R. (1971). The aim of psychoanalysis. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 52, 103–106] which he had previously denied, with the aim of integrating past and present, inner fantasy and outer reality, and the various experiences of self that he has made in his various countries.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflective functioning in patients with obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) – preliminary findings of a comparison between reflective functioning (RF) in general and OCD-specific reflective functioning

Niclas Kullgard , Per Persson , Clara Möller , Fredrik Falkenström and Rolf Holmqvist

Rolf Holmqvist

Linköping
Sweden
Department of Behavioral Sciences and Learning

Rolf Holmqvist  

obsessive-compulsive disorder, reflective function, mentalization

Several scales for rating the reflective function (RF) in different psychiatric syndromes (symptom-specific reflective function [SRF]) have been developed. In this study, the validity of a rating instrument for measuring obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD)-specific RF (OCD-SRF) was assessed. The study was conducted at two outpatient psychiatric facilities. A total of 18 women and 12 men were interviewed with adult attachment interview and the OCD–SRF interview. The patients also rated their symptoms on a self-rating scale (Yale-Brown Obsessive–Compulsive Scale [Y-BOCS]). The results indicated that there was a significant difference between the levels of RF in general and SRF. No correlations between Y-BOCS and RF or SRF were found. Illustrations of ratings are presented. The difference between RF and SRF, the relationship between SRF and symptom checklists, and a relational perspective on symptoms in OCD were discussed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The silent child: communication without words

Joan Symington

Joan Symington

Sydney
Australia

Joan Symington  

Jeanne Magagna, Karnac Books, 2012, 366 pp. (paperback and eBook)

Over the past three decades, there has been increasing awareness of children and young people who have given up on struggling with life and have stopped speaking, eating and walking. This pervasive ‘retreat from life’ is based on extreme anxiety about growing up and the changes involved, which are inevitably painful at times. Change itself is dreaded and these children attempt to control and stop change though their apparent resistance to living.

Jeanne Magagna, from her position of Head of Psychotherapy Services at Great Ormond Street Hospital for Sick Children in London and later working at the Ellern Mede Centre for children with eating disorders, has seen and treated 15 of these children and young people and is a pioneer in this field. It requires great determination, faith and courage to treat these extremely difficult and disturbed children, and reliance on the multidisciplinary team is mandatory. This new book of Magagna's is the first really comprehensive account of psychodynamic treatment to emerge. It is a multidisciplinary endeavour with multiple authors, many of whom are members of the team. Thus, the authors of chapters include a teacher, a physiotherapist, an art therapist, a psychology student and a family therapist, each describing their personal intensive work with a child. There is also a heart-rending chapter written by the bewildered mother of one of these pervasively refusing children.

The book is written not only for professionals but also for the parents of these children for whom the condition, and the treatment, may be a source of great pain, but whose assistance is absolutely essential in the treatment.

Knowing the importance of detailed observation from doing infant observation with Esther Bick, Magagna encouraged the nurses at Great Ormond Street Hospital to observe these children closely and to note the minute by minute personal experience of being with them, and in this way to learn to see through their eyes. She also suggested that the nurses try to imagine what it was like to be the parents of such a child. The nurses would discuss what they had observed including their own feelings aroused by being with the child. Multidisciplinary discussion groups were also a great help and support in continuing to work with these children who gave such negative, apparently non-responsive feedback.

Being present for each other: long-distance video-linked seminars

Jeanne Magagna

Jeanne Magagna

Jeanne Magagna   *Email: jm@hoping.demon.co.uk
long-distance learning, video-linked seminars, international, infant observation

This paper describes technical issues in setting up and using long-distance learning equipment. It also describes aspects of long-distance learning during stages of the learning process and evaluations of the method. The use of the countertransference is also discussed as an important aspect of the work.
